title: Gynecomastia from Systemic, Endocrine & Nutritional Disorders: A Case-Based Atlas
subtitle: Nine published cases — organ failure (cirrhosis, CKD), endocrine disease (Graves, acromegaly, feminizing adrenal carcinoma, GH therapy) and iatrogenic/nutritional causes (refeeding, isoniazid, efavirenz)
authors: Resident training module — synthesis of published case reports (see sourceFile); original teaching text. All cited cases verified against PubMed/PMC.
sourceFile: Gynecomastia_Systemic_Etiologies_Resident_Training_Module.md, with every case citation verified against PubMed/PMC: cirrhosis — Gordon GG et al., Ann Intern Med 1973;79:198–203; CKD/ESRD mechanism — Schmitt GW et al., Ann Intern Med 1968;69:73–79; Graves — Endocrinol Diabetes Metab Case Rep 2021, EDM20-0181 (PMC8115411); acromegaly — Lim F & Rao A, J Endocr Soc 2022 (PMC9625425); GH therapy — Acharya SV et al., Indian J Pediatr 2010 (PMID 20140778) & Malozowski S, Stadel BV, J Pediatr 1995 (PMID 7699552); feminizing ACC — Umphonsathien et al., Kaohsiung J Med Sci 2018;34:705–706 & Alruwaili et al., Am J Case Rep 2022 (PMC9801065); isoniazid — Khan AA & Agarwal R, Lung India 2012;29:189–191 (PMC3354502) & Morrone et al., J Bras Pneumol 2008;34:978–981; efavirenz — van Ramshorst MS et al., BMC Pediatr 2013;13:120 (PMC3751361). AI-generated objective items (carrying distillation_trace) were re-validated; malformed option labels stripped and one off-topic (hepatotoxicity) duplicate item removed.
caseDescription: Case-based deep dive covering systemic / organ-failure, endocrine, and nutritional/iatrogenic etiologies of gynecomastia.
learningObjectives: Understand the mechanical disruption of the free testosterone-to-estrogen ratio
Utilize a master diagnostic algorithm for true gynecomastia

items.title: CASE 1 — Feminization & gynecomastia in Laennec's hepatic cirrhosis (adult male)
items.content:
Middle-aged man with biopsy-proven alcohol-related (Laennec's) cirrhosis presents with bilateral breast enlargement, reduced libido, and loss of body hair. He reports years of heavy alcohol use.

Examination shows stigmata of chronic liver disease: spider angiomata, palmar erythema, testicular atrophy, sparse body hair, bilateral firm subareolar glandular tissue (true gynecomastia).

Investigations show low total & free testosterone, low dihydrotestosterone, high total & free estradiol, supranormal E2:testosterone ratio, elevated LH, normal FSH.

items.title: How cirrhosis raises the estrogen:androgen ratio — mechanism and the spironolactone trap
items.content:
The uniformly elevated E2:T ratio drives gynecomastia. Cirrhosis raises SHBG (binds testosterone avidly, lowering free T), and there is increased extraglandular aromatization of androstenedione whose hepatic clearance is impaired.

Management: Treat the liver disease and abstinence from alcohol. Critical pitfall: spironolactone (used for ascites) independently causes painful gynecomastia by displacing E2 from SHBG and inhibiting 17β-HSD type 2; substituting eplerenone reduces painful gynecomastia.

(Case basis: Gordon GG, Olivo J, Rafii F, Southren AL, Ann Intern Med 1973;79:198–203, who documented the uniformly supranormal estradiol:testosterone ratio in cirrhotic men.)

items.question: Why can total testosterone look 'normal' in a cirrhotic with obvious feminization?
items.answer: Cirrhosis often triggers an increase in Sex Hormone-Binding Globulin (SHBG) synthesis. Because SHBG binds testosterone with significantly higher affinity than it binds estradiol, total testosterone may appear normal while the biologically active free fraction is severely depleted.
keyPoints: SHBG binds testosterone with ~5× higher affinity than estradiol.
Increased SHBG lowers free testosterone, raising the free E2:T ratio even when totals look normal.

items.question: Your cirrhotic patient on spironolactone develops new, painful gynecomastia — what is your first move?
items.answer: Substitute spironolactone with eplerenone to reduce painful gynecomastia.

items.question: Which estrogen (E1 vs E2) best tracks cirrhosis severity?
items.answer: Severe disease (higher MELD / Child-Pugh B–C) correlates with greater estrogen excess, especially estrone (E1).

items.title: CASE 2 — Bilateral gynecomastia in a hemodialysis-dependent man (CKD/ESRD)
items.content:
A 42-year-old man with ESRD secondary to mesangial IgA nephropathy, on maintenance hemodialysis, presents with bilateral gynecomastia and impaired sexual function.

Examination shows bilateral firm retroareolar glandular tissue; small/soft testes; no discrete hard mass; no nipple discharge.

Investigations show normal gonadotropins (LH/FSH), markedly elevated prolactin (4285 mU/L), pituitary MRI shows no microadenoma; arachnoidocele present. Breast ultrasound shows bilateral nodular gynecomastia, no suspicious lesion.

items.title: Why ESRD causes gynecomastia — uremic hypogonadism, hyperprolactinemia and a refeeding effect
items.content:
ESRD produces uremic hypogonadism often with elevated LH/FSH, plus hyperprolactinemia from reduced renal clearance. A refeeding-like mechanism also operates: dialysis improves the malnourished, catabolic state, reactivating the gonadal axis — historically likened to post-WWII 'refeeding gynecomastia'.

Management: Hyperprolactinemic patient treated with bromocriptine. Exclude iatrogenic causes (CCBs, PPIs, ACE inhibitors, spironolactone, H2 blockers, darbepoetin). Dialysis-associated gynecomastia often resolves spontaneously in 1–2 years; renal transplantation most reliably corrects both hormonal and nutritional drivers.

(Mechanistic basis: Schmitt GW, Shehadeh I, Sawin CT, Ann Intern Med 1968;69:73–79.)

items.question: Gynecomastia appears in a malnourished ESRD patient after starting effective dialysis — why?
items.answer: Dialysis improves the malnourished, catabolic state, reactivating the gonadal axis (refeeding-like mechanism), which can cause a transient surge in the estrogen-to-androgen ratio.

items.question: How does CKD elevate prolactin independent of a pituitary adenoma?
items.answer: Reduced renal clearance in CKD reduces prolactin clearance. Pituitary stalk/sellar abnormalities can also add a disconnection component.

items.question: Which single intervention most reliably resolves CKD-related gynecomastia?
items.answer: Renal transplantation most reliably corrects both hormonal and nutritional drivers.

items.title: CASE 3 — Gynecomastia as the presenting symptom of untreated Graves' disease
items.content:
A 49-year-old man presents with right-sided gynecomastia. Directed history reveals additional thyrotoxic symptoms (heat intolerance, palpitations, weight change).

Examination shows healthy-appearing man, fine tremor, slightly enlarged non-tender thyroid; bilateral gynecomastia (right > left), Tanner M2; normal-sized testes.

Investigations show suppressed TSH, high free T4, high Anti-TSH receptor antibody, markedly high SHBG (143 nmol/L), normal total testosterone, low calculated free testosterone, normal estradiol.

items.title: How thyrotoxicosis drives gynecomastia via aromatase and SHBG
items.content:
Thyroid hormone stimulates peripheral aromatase, raising estrogen, and increases hepatic SHBG synthesis, which lowers free testosterone while total T stays normal. Net effect: increased free E2 : free T ratio. LH may also rise and further drive aromatization.

Management: Antithyroid drug (thiamazole/methimazole) ± later levothyroxine, or radioiodine. Gynecomastia and other symptoms resolved completely within ~3 months of restoring euthyroidism. SHBG normalized and calculated free T rose into normal range as FT4/T3 fell.

(Case basis: Endocrinol Diabetes Metab Case Rep 2021, EDM20-0181; PMC8115411.)

items.question: Why order a TSH in every new gynecomastia workup, even when the patient looks well?
items.answer: Gynecomastia can be the presenting symptom of hyperthyroidism, and a significant proportion of thyrotoxic men may have gynecomastia. Unrecognized hyperthyroidism is a treatable cause.

items.question: How can a thyrotoxic man have normal total but low free testosterone?
items.answer: Thyroid hormone increases hepatic SHBG synthesis. Increased SHBG binds testosterone, reducing the calculated free testosterone even while total testosterone is normal.

items.title: CASE 4 — Bilateral gynecomastia as the initial presentation of acromegaly (GH/PRL-cosecreting macroadenoma)
items.content:
A 50-year-old man with hypertension, hyperlipidemia, pre-diabetes, carpal tunnel syndrome, and obstructive sleep apnea is referred for 5 months of bilateral gynecomastia, with chronic fatigue and erectile dysfunction.

Examination: Larger, tender left breast; no nipple discharge. Large hands and feet, skin tags.

Investigations: High Prolactin (49.3 ng/mL), low Total testosterone (AM) (186 ng/dL), inappropriately normal-low LH/FSH (secondary hypogonadism), high IGF-1 (585 ng/mL), GH after OGTT >2.4 ng/mL (non-suppressed). Pituitary MRI shows 2.3 cm macroadenoma.

items.title: How a GH/prolactin macroadenoma produces gynecomastia
items.content:
A somatotroph (often GH/PRL) macroadenoma causes (1) hyperprolactinemia (PRL cosecreted in ~25% of acromegaly) → secondary hypogonadism and possibly direct breast effects, and (2) GH/IGF-1 actions on breast tissue. The resulting low testosterone + relative estrogen effect produces gynecomastia.

Management: transsphenoidal hypophysectomy. Post-op: prolactin, IGF-1, and testosterone normalized; gynecomastia completely resolved.

(Case basis: Lim F & Rao A, J Endocr Soc 2022; PMC9625425 — the same 50-year-old man, prolactin 49.3, IGF-1 585, 2.3 cm adenoma, gynecomastia resolved after transsphenoidal surgery.)

items.question: A man with new gynecomastia, hyperprolactinemia, and carpal tunnel + acral changes — what single imaging study and what confirmatory test do you order?
items.answer: Pituitary MRI and GH after OGTT.

items.question: Why is an elevated IGF-1 not sufficient alone to confirm GH excess in some settings, and what is the gold-standard dynamic test?
items.answer: IGF-1 is a screening test; the gold-standard confirmatory test is GH non-suppression after an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT). Elevated IGF-1 can sometimes be non-specific or affected by other conditions.

items.title: CASE 5 — Prepubertal gynecomastia during growth hormone (GH) treatment
items.content:
A prepubertal boy on recombinant GH (for GH deficiency / short stature) develops breast tissue ~6 months after starting GH. No exogenous estrogen exposure; no other new drugs.

Examination shows prepubertal genitalia (testes <4 mL, no other pubertal signs) with palpable subareolar glandular tissue — i.e., gynecomastia dissociated from true puberty.

Investigations show prepubertal LH/FSH and testosterone, normal/low estradiol, negative workup for adrenal/testicular tumor, CAH.

items.title: Why GH therapy can cause benign, self-limited prepubertal gynecomastia
items.content:
GH/IGF-1 may directly sensitize or stimulate breast glandular tissue and modulate local aromatase/steroidogenesis. The condition is self-limited and benign.

Management: Reassurance and observation. Gynecomastia often resolves spontaneously without stopping GH.

(Case basis: Acharya SV et al., Indian J Pediatr 2010;77:443–444, PMID 20140778; pharmacovigilance: Malozowski S & Stadel BV, J Pediatr 1995;126:659–661, PMID 7699552.)

items.question: How do you distinguish GH-treatment gynecomastia from the onset of true puberty in a treated boy?
items.answer: Check pubertal staging (testicular volume) and LH/FSH/testosterone levels. In GH-treatment gynecomastia, testes remain prepubertal (<4 mL) and hormones are prepubertal.

items.question: What pathologic causes of prepubertal gynecomastia must be excluded before attributing it to GH (think tumors and enzyme defects)?
items.answer: Adrenal adenoma/carcinoma, testicular tumor (incl. LCCSCT), 11β-hydroxylase deficiency, familial aromatase excess.

items.title: CASE 6 — Painful gynecomastia revealing Cushing syndrome (feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma)
items.content:
A 43-year-old man presents with gynecomastia for 4 months, loss of libido, and 3-kg weight gain.

Examination shows bilateral gynecomastia with testicular atrophy, Cushingoid appearance, and a palpable abdominal mass.

Investigations show high 24-h urinary free cortisol, low ACTH, markedly high estradiol (1221 pmol/L). CT abdomen shows 12×13×15 cm left suprarenal mass; lung metastases.

items.title: How a feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma causes both gynecomastia and Cushing syndrome
items.content:
A feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma (ACC) secretes both cortisol (→ ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome) and estrogen, the latter from increased tumoral aromatase activity and peripheral conversion of tumor-derived weak androgens.

Management: Left adrenalectomy + nephrectomy + IVC thrombectomy; mitotane for advanced disease. Estradiol fell and gynecomastia regressed post-operatively.

(Case basis: Umphonsathien et al., Kaohsiung J Med Sci 2018;34:705–706; Carney-complex screen: Alruwaili et al., Am J Case Rep 2022, PMC9801065.)

items.question: New gynecomastia + Cushingoid features + an abdominal mass — what is the unifying diagnosis until proven otherwise?
items.answer: Feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma (ACC).

items.question: How do you biochemically separate ACTH-dependent from ACTH-independent Cushing syndrome at the bedside lab level?
items.answer: Measure ACTH. Low/suppressed ACTH indicates ACTH-independent Cushing (e.g., adrenal tumor); high/normal ACTH indicates ACTH-dependent (e.g., pituitary or ectopic).

items.question: In a child with gynecomastia + Cushing + lentigines + testicular tumor, what syndrome must you screen for, and which gene?
items.answer: Carney complex (PRKAR1A gene mutation).

items.title: CASE 7 — Refeeding gynecomastia during recovery from chronic illness
items.content:
A previously malnourished man — recovering from prolonged catabolic illness (e.g., severe chronic disease/anorexia) — develops bilateral, often tender gynecomastia weeks to months after nutritional repletion begins.

Examination shows bilateral, frequently tender subareolar glandular tissue; recovering nutritional status (weight regain); no abdominal mass; testes normal/recovering.

items.title: Why refeeding triggers a transient 'second puberty' gynecomastia
items.content:
Malnutrition suppresses gonadotropin secretion → hypogonadism. With refeeding, the gonadal axis reactivates; the return of gonadotropin and gonadal function produces a transient estrogen:androgen imbalance that mimics puberty ('refeeding gynecomastia'). Resolves spontaneously in 1–2 years.

Management: Reassurance and continued nutritional rehabilitation. Exclude organ failure, drugs, and tumors first.

(The classic post-war 'refeeding gynecomastia' first described in returning, previously starved prisoners of war.)

items.question: Why does feeding a starved patient — not starving them — trigger gynecomastia?
items.answer: Refeeding reactivates the suppressed gonadal axis, causing a transient estrogen:androgen imbalance similar to a 'second puberty'.

items.title: CASE 8 — Isoniazid (antitubercular therapy)–induced gynecomastia
items.content:
A 45-year-old man with tubercular cervical lymphadenitis on DOTS (rifampicin + isoniazid + pyrazinamide) develops a painful, progressively enlarging left breast swelling after ~3 months of ATT.

Examination shows a firm ~4 cm mound of tissue around the left nipple-areola complex (true gynecomastia); normal genitalia, thyroid, and secondary sexual characteristics.

Investigations show normal hormone panel, liver, renal, thyroid function. Ultrasound shows true gynecomastia. Causality: Probable — temporal link + dechallenge response.

items.title: How isoniazid is implicated in gynecomastia — and the proof from rechallenge
items.content:
Mechanism is uncertain; proposed: isoniazid-induced disturbance of pyridoxine (vitamin B6) metabolism altering the androgen–estrogen balance, and/or a refeeding-type physiology during recovery from the chronic wasting illness of TB.

Management: withdrawal of isoniazid leads to resolution (often over months to ~1 year). A recent unilateral case responded to increasing pyridoxine with reassurance.

(Case basis and systematic review: Khan AA & Agarwal R, Lung India 2012;29:189–191, PMC3354502; the rechallenge proof: Morrone et al., J Bras Pneumol 2008;34:978–981 — gynecomastia recurred on retreatment 8 years later.)

items.question: Which single drug in the standard RHZE regimen is the usual culprit, and what is the typical time-to-onset?
items.answer: Isoniazid, with typical time-to-onset of 3-5 months.

items.question: What clinical 'experiment of nature' in the Morrone case essentially proved causation?
items.answer: Recurrence on rechallenge 8 years later.

items.title: CASE 9 — Efavirenz (antiretroviral)–induced gynecomastia
items.content:
A 29-year-old man on efavirenz + lamivudine + tenofovir DF develops progressive bilateral gynecomastia ~2 years into ART, ultimately reaching grade III by 6 years. A 7-year-old girl developed true gynecomastia 4 months after starting abacavir/lamivudine/efavirenz.

Examination shows true glandular breast enlargement. Must be distinguished from pseudogynecomastia/lipodystrophy caused by NRTIs.

Investigations show normal hormones, liver/renal/thyroid function. Breast ultrasound confirms true gynecomastia.

items.title: Why early recognition of efavirenz gynecomastia changes the outcome
items.content:
Efavirenz is the antiretroviral most frequently implicated in true gynecomastia. 

Management: In pediatric girl, efavirenz replaced with nevirapine → complete resolution by ~6 weeks. In adult man, symptoms unreported for years → reached grade III → required bilateral mastectomy. Early recognition allows reversibility.

(Case basis: van Ramshorst MS et al., BMC Pediatr 2013;13:120, PMC3751361 — a child switched to nevirapine with resolution; chronic untreated adult disease reached grade III requiring mastectomy.)

items.question: How do you differentiate efavirenz-induced true gynecomastia from NRTI lipodystrophy on exam/imaging — and why does it matter for management?
items.answer: Breast ultrasound showing retroareolar glandular tissue differentiates true gynecomastia from lipodystrophy (which is fat redistribution). This is critical to avoid inappropriately switching drugs.

items.question: Why is early recognition critical to outcome?
items.answer: Early recognition allows for reversibility upon drug switching, whereas chronic untreated cases may reach advanced grades requiring surgery.

items.stem: A cirrhotic man on spironolactone for ascites develops new painful gynecomastia. The best next step is:
items.explanation: Spironolactone is a classic cause; eplerenone substitution reduces painful gynecomastia.

items.stem: Which lab abnormality is uniformly present in cirrhotic men with gynecomastia (Gordon 1973)?
items.explanation: A supranormal estradiol:testosterone ratio is present in all cases.

items.stem: Gynecomastia developing 2–9 months after starting hemodialysis in a previously malnourished patient is best explained by:
options: Refeeding-type reactivation of the gonadal axis
items.explanation: Dialysis improves the malnourished state, leading to a refeeding-type reactivation of the gonadal axis.

items.stem: In thyrotoxic gynecomastia, total testosterone may be normal but free testosterone is low because:
items.explanation: Thyrotoxicosis markedly increases SHBG, which binds testosterone, lowering the free fraction.

items.stem: The gold-standard confirmatory test for acromegaly is:
items.explanation: IGF-1 screens; OGTT-GH confirms; MRI cannot distinguish functioning tumors.

items.stem: Prepubertal gynecomastia appearing months after starting GH therapy is generally:
options: A sign of malignancy requiring biopsy
An absolute indication to stop GH
items.explanation: Benign and often self-limited, but pathologic causes must first be excluded.

items.stem: A 43-year-old man with gynecomastia, Cushingoid features, very high estradiol, low ACTH, and a large adrenal mass most likely has:
items.explanation: Feminizing adrenocortical carcinoma is the unifying diagnosis.

items.stem: A child with gynecomastia, Cushing syndrome, lentigines, and a calcifying Sertoli cell tumor should be tested for a mutation in:
items.explanation: Carney complex (PRKAR1A).

items.stem: The strongest evidence that isoniazid caused a patient's gynecomastia is:
options: Recurrence on rechallenge with the same drug
items.explanation: Recurrence on rechallenge proves cause (Morrone case).

items.stem: In an HIV patient on efavirenz with breast enlargement, the most important distinction before changing therapy is:
items.explanation: Breast US showing retroareolar glandular tissue confirms true gynecomastia.

items.stem: Why can total testosterone levels appear normal in a cirrhotic patient with clinical feminization?
options: Cirrhosis decreases SHBG synthesis, leading to increased free testosterone.
Cirrhosis increases SHBG synthesis, which binds testosterone with higher affinity than estradiol, reducing free testosterone.
Liver dysfunction directly converts testosterone to estradiol, lowering total testosterone.
Portal hypertension reduces testosterone production by the testes.
items.explanation: Cirrhosis increases SHBG synthesis. SHBG binds testosterone with ~5× higher affinity than estradiol, lowering free testosterone and increasing the free E2:T ratio despite normal total testosterone levels.

items.stem: A cirrhotic patient on spironolactone develops new, painful gynecomastia. What is the most appropriate initial intervention?
items.explanation: Eplerenone is a selective aldosterone antagonist with fewer androgenic effects compared to spironolactone, making it a better choice for patients developing gynecomastia.

items.stem: Which estrogen (E1 vs E2) is most strongly associated with cirrhosis severity as indicated by higher MELD or Child-Pugh scores?
options: Estradiol (E2) is more closely correlated with cirrhosis severity
Testosterone levels are the best biomarker for cirrhosis progression
Estrone (E1) shows greater correlation with severe cirrhosis
Progesterone levels are inversely related to liver dysfunction
items.explanation: Estrone (E1) is associated with greater estrogen excess in severe cirrhosis (higher MELD/Child-Pugh B–C), reflecting altered hepatic metabolism and increased aromatase activity in advanced liver disease.

items.stem: Which single intervention most reliably resolves CKD-related gynecomastia?
items.explanation: Renal transplantation addresses both the hormonal dysregulation and nutritional deficiencies underlying CKD-related gynecomastia, unlike other interventions that only manage symptoms.

items.stem: A thyrotoxic man may have normal total testosterone but low free testosterone due to which of the following mechanisms?
options: Decreased testosterone production by Leydig cells
Increased hepatic synthesis of sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG)
Increased conversion of testosterone to estrogen
items.explanation: Thyroid hormone stimulates hepatic SHBG synthesis, which binds testosterone more tightly, reducing calculated free testosterone levels despite normal total testosterone concentrations.

IGF-1 is a screening test; the gold standard is GH suppression after an oral glucose tolerance test (OGTT)
items.explanation: IGF-1 is a screening tool but can be non-specific; GH non-suppression after OGTT is the gold-standard confirmatory test for GH excess.

items.stem: How should gynecomastia induced by growth hormone (GH) treatment be differentiated from the onset of true puberty in a male patient?
options: Assess breast size and symmetry; GH-induced gynecomastia is typically unilateral

Monitor response to anti-estrogen therapy; GH-induced gynecomastia resolves more rapidly
items.explanation: GH-induced gynecomastia occurs in the absence of pubertal testicular enlargement (testicular volume <4 mL) and with prepubertal hormone levels, whereas true puberty is associated with testicular growth and elevated testosterone.

items.title: How to work up gynecomastia from systemic disease — a master algorithm
items.content:
Confirm true glandular tissue (firm, concentric, subareolar) and exclude male breast cancer (hard, eccentric, fixed mass, bloody discharge → imaging/biopsy). Then run a tiered work-up that maps onto the buckets in this atlas.

ASCII flow:
New gynecomastia
  ├─ Drugs/exposure? → review meds (spironolactone, etc.), dechallenge
  ├─ Organ failure? → LFTs/MELD (cirrhosis), creatinine/eGFR (CKD)
  ├─ TSH/free T4 → Graves (high SHBG, normal total / low free T)
  ├─ IGF-1 (+ acral/CTS features) → OGTT-GH → acromegaly (MRI)
  ├─ Estradiol very high + Cushingoid + abdominal mass → feminizing ACC (CT, low ACTH)
  ├─ Prolactin → prolactinoma / CKD-related hyperprolactinaemia
  └─ hCG, LH/FSH, total+free testosterone, E2 → hypogonadism/tumour screen

Key discriminators: a high SHBG with low calculated free testosterone points to thyrotoxicosis; a markedly high estradiol with a low ACTH and an adrenal mass points to feminizing ACC; an inappropriately normal/low LH with low testosterone points to a central (pituitary) cause such as acromegaly with cosecreted prolactin.
keyPoints: Confirm glandular tissue and exclude breast cancer first
TSH, IGF-1, estradiol, ACTH, prolactin, hCG and LH/FSH/testosterone each discriminate a different bucket
High SHBG → thyrotoxicosis; high E2 + low ACTH + adrenal mass → feminizing ACC; low LH + low T → central/acromegaly

items.title: Why these systemic-gynecomastia cases carry citation and evidence caveats
items.content:
Strength: this atlas links nine distinct systemic, endocrine and iatrogenic causes to one final common pathway — a raised effective estrogen:androgen ratio — and every named case has been verified against PubMed/PMC (cirrhosis: Gordon 1973; CKD mechanism: Schmitt 1968; Graves: PMC8115411; acromegaly: Lim & Rao, J Endocr Soc 2022, PMC9625425; GH therapy: Acharya 2010 + Malozowski 1995; feminizing ACC: Umphonsathien 2018 + Alruwaili 2022; isoniazid: Khan & Agarwal 2012 + Morrone 2008; efavirenz: van Ramshorst 2013).

Caveats and doubt-flags: (1) Several of the per-case biochemical values (e.g. the CKD case's prolactin of 4285 mU/L with an arachnoidocele, the ACC case's estradiol of 1221 pmol/L) are illustrative figures carried from the source compilation and were not each re-extracted from the primary papers — treat them as teaching numbers, not exact quotations. (2) The cirrhosis and uraemia primary data are from the 1960s–70s and predate modern free-hormone assays. (3) Drug attributions (isoniazid, efavirenz) rest on temporal association and dechallenge/rechallenge, not controlled trials. (4) A subset of the objective questions in this module were generated by a local AI model — their raw chain-of-thought is preserved verbatim in each item's distillation_trace; malformed option labels were stripped and one off-topic (hepatotoxicity) duplicate was removed during review. Statements here are educational, not prescriptive.
keyPoints: Every named case is PubMed/PMC-verified; the final common pathway is a raised estrogen:androgen ratio
Some per-case lab values are illustrative teaching figures, not exact primary-source quotes (doubt-flag)
AI-generated objective items retain their distillation_trace; labels cleaned and one off-topic item removed
